text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY PROGRESS NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review and Oncology Follow-Up
Patient: Raymond Kowalski | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Oncologist, Hematology/Oncology]
Co-signers: Hepatobiliary Surgery, Interventional Radiology (tumor board representation)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male with known Stage IV (cT3 N2a M1a) KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar liver metastases, here for multidisciplinary tumor board re-review and oncology follow-up following completion of 8 cycles of modified FOLFOX-6 plus Avastin. Primary question today: reassessment of hepatic resectability in light of imaging response.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 4 months ago presenting with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases (4 lesions, largest 3.2 cm in Segment VI right lobe). KRAS G12D mutant, BRAF wild-type, pMMR/MSS by IHC. Given his presentation with bilobar liver disease, the original multidisciplinary tumor board had recommended conversion chemotherapy with intent to downsize the hepatic burden and reassess surgical candidacy. He initiated mFOLFOX-6 plus Avastin 5 mg/kg biweekly and has now completed 8 cycles.

Interim imaging after Cycle 4 showed a partial response with approximately 28% reduction in hepatic disease. CEA had fallen from 148.2 at baseline to 67.4 at that timepoint. The case was discussed at tumor board after Cycle 4 with the decision to continue chemotherapy for 4 additional cycles before formal surgical reassessment.

He now presents for that formal reassessment. Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (post-Cycle 8) shows continued hepatic response: the dominant Segment VI lesion has decreased to 2.0 x 1.7 cm (from 3.2 x 2.8 cm at baseline), Segment IVb to 0.9 x 0.8 cm, Segment II to 0.7 x 0.6 cm, and the Segment VII lesion now measures only 0.5 x 0.4 cm -- near-radiographic complete response in the smaller lesions. Sigmoid primary has also continued to shrink (now 3.1 x 2.7 cm vs. 4.1 x 3.8 cm at baseline). No new sites of distant disease. CEA trending favorably at 38.1 ng/mL, down from 148.2 at baseline.

From a tolerability standpoint, he developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy after Cycle 7 (cumulative Eloxatin effect). He has bilateral persistent numbness and tingling in hands and feet at rest, with decreased vibratory sense in the toes and mild difficulty with fine motor tasks. oxali dose was reduced 25% (to 64 mg/m2) at that time per protocol. Blood pressure is currently well-controlled at 134/82 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg daily (dose had been uptitrated from 10 mg due to bev-related Grade 1 hypertension earlier in the course). He also notes mild Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome and Grade 1 alopecia, both non-dose-limiting.

Today's tumor board included colorectal oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, interventional radiology, and diagnostic radiology. Discussion detailed below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Bilateral hand and foot numbness/tingling (neuropathy, chronic, stable to slightly improved from nadir); mild fatigue (Grade 1); mild intermittent nausea (well-controlled); thinning of scalp hair. Negative: No fever, chills, night sweats. No new or worsening abdominal pain. No jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, or hepatic symptoms. No melena, hematochezia, or change in bowel habits. No dyspnea, chest pain, or cough. No lower extremity edema. No headache or dizziness. Appetite moderately preserved; mild weight loss over course of treatment (3 lbs over 4 months, patient attributes to dietary changes and treatment-related nausea).

---

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vital Signs: BP 134/82 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, Temp 98.4F, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 179 lbs (BMI 27.1).
General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears his stated age. Mild diffuse alopecia noted.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mild thinning of scalp hair. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in left lower quadrant (over sigmoid tumor site), without guarding or rigidity. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. No ascites.
Chest: Right chest wall port site clean, dry, intact -- no erythema, warmth, or induration. Port accessed without difficulty prior to today's infusion.
Extremities: No cyanosis. No lower extremity edema bilaterally. Bilateral mild skin changes consistent with Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome (erythema at fingertip pads and plantar surfaces, no blistering).
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Decreased vibratory sense at bilateral great toes (present but diminished). Light touch reduced in bilateral fingertips and plantar surfaces. Gait steady. Romberg negative.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

---

LAB RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 5.2 K/uL, Hgb 10.9 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, stable), MCV 88, Platelets 214 K/uL, ANC 2.8 K/uL.
CMP: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60). ALT 61 U/L (mildly elevated, likely hepatic disease effect), AST 55 U/L, Total Bili 0.8, ALP 112 (mildly elevated), Albumin 3.6 g/dL.
Tumor Markers: CEA 38.1 ng/mL (down from 67.4 post-Cycle 4, down from 148.2 at baseline). CA 19-9 not checked today (checked at last imaging timepoint).
Coagulation: INR 1.1 (noted for surgical planning context).
Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.14 (acceptable, within bev monitoring threshold).

---

IMAGING REVIEW:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast (post-Cycle 8): Reviewed with diagnostic radiology at tumor board.
- Sigmoid colon primary: 3.1 x 2.7 cm (from 4.1 x 3.8 cm at baseline; approximately 24% linear reduction in largest dimension).
- Liver Segment VI: 2.0 x 1.7 cm (from 3.2 x 2.8 cm baseline; 37% reduction).
- Liver Segment IVb: 0.9 x 0.8 cm (from 1.8 x 1.6 cm baseline; near-50% reduction).
- Liver Segment II: 0.7 x 0.6 cm (from 1.4 x 1.1 cm baseline; 50% reduction).
- Liver Segment VII: 0.5 x 0.4 cm (from 0.9 x 0.8 cm; substantially reduced, near-complete radiographic response).
- No new hepatic lesions. No pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal disease. No lymphadenopathy.
Overall hepatic tumor burden significantly reduced from baseline. Hepatic parenchymal quality: No imaging features of sinusoidal obstruction syndrome or chemotherapy-associated steatohepatitis (CASH) identified; however, hepatic parenchymal changes with 8-cycle Eloxatin exposure a known concern.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION:
Present: Colorectal Oncology, Hepatobiliary Surgery, Interventional Radiology, Diagnostic Radiology.

Hepatic Surgery Assessment (Dr. [Hepatobiliary Surgery]):
Technical resectability was reviewed in detail. The Segment VI lesion (2.0 x 1.7 cm) is the dominant remaining hepatic lesion and is technically resectable with a planned posterior right hepatectomy approach. However, the residual bilobar nature of disease (Segments VI, IVb, II, and VII all involved -- though the latter is near-complete radiographic response) continues to pose a challenge for a curative-intent single-stage resection. To achieve R0 margins on all four sites would require an extended right hepatectomy with resection of the segment II/IVb lesions, likely necessitating a two-stage hepatectomy approach or combination of hepatectomy with radiofrequency ablation (RFA) of smaller contralateral lesions.

Future liver remnant (FLR) estimate based on current volumetric imaging: approximately 28% of total liver volume. This is at the lower threshold of acceptable FLR (generally >25-30% in a non-cirrhotic liver with chemotherapy exposure), but the team noted that cumulative FOLFOX hepatotoxicity -- specifically oxali-associated sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS) -- is a real risk at 8 cycles, even without overt imaging correlates. A pre-operative liver biopsy or intraoperative assessment of parenchymal quality would be warranted if resection proceeds.

Portal vein embolization (PVE) of the right portal vein was discussed as an option to augment FLR hypertrophy if required prior to right hepatectomy. However, given the continued favorable treatment response and the availability of 4 more planned cycles, the surgical team felt it premature to commit to PVE at this time.

For the Segment II lesion and the near-resolved Segment VII lesion, image-guided ablation (RFA or microwave ablation) could be considered as an adjunct to formal hepatic resection, potentially making a planned two-stage approach unnecessary depending on response at the end of 12 total cycles.

Conclusion from surgical perspective: Resection not recommended at this juncture given marginal FLR, bilobar involvement, and ongoing chemotherapy response. Recommend completing planned 12-cycle course and reassessing with formal volumetric imaging and surgical consultation at that time.

Oncology Assessment:
Patient has shown robust and continued treatment response over 8 cycles. CEA downtrend remains favorable. The case for continuing oxali-based therapy to maximize hepatic response is strong; however, Grade 2 neuropathy at dose-reduced Eloxatin is a real concern for cumulative toxicity with 4 additional cycles, even at 64 mg/m2. Will monitor closely. Avastin to continue, as no significant proteinuria or non-healing wound concerns.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. Stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar liver metastases -- ONGOING PARTIAL RESPONSE to mFOLFOX-6/Avastin after 8 cycles.
   - Continued significant downtrending of hepatic burden and sigmoid primary. Cumulative 42% decrease in measured disease from baseline.
   - CEA 38.1, continued favorable trajectory.
   - Tumor board consensus: Continue mFOLFOX-6 + Avastin for 4 additional cycles (total 12 cycles) as planned. Do NOT proceed to surgical resection at this time.
   - Formal hepatic resectability reassessment after Cycle 12 with dedicated hepatic volumetry and repeat hepatobiliary surgery consultation.
   - Portal vein embolization to be reconsidered at that time if FLR remains marginal. Ablative approach (RFA) for residual contralateral lesions under active consideration as adjunct strategy.
   - Primary tumor remains unresected and symptomatic monitoring ongoing (currently asymptomatic).

2. Grade 2 oxali-induced peripheral neuropathy.
   - Eloxatin ongoing at dose-reduced 64 mg/m2 (25% reduction from 85 mg/m2).
   - Will continue at this dose for the remaining 4 cycles. If neuropathy worsens to persistent Grade 2 with functional impairment or progresses to Grade 3, oxali will be discontinued entirely; 5-FU and Avastin to continue.
   - Neurology follow-up maintained. Patient counseled on ongoing cold avoidance and use of gloves for cold exposure.
   - Anticipate improvement in neuropathy following Eloxatin discontinuation at cycle 12.

3. bev-related hypertension (previously Grade 1, now controlled).
   - BP today 134/82 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg daily. Acceptable.
   - Continue current antihypertensive regimen. Monitor BP at each visit.
   - Urine protein:creatinine 0.14, within acceptable range. Continue monitoring.

4. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 10.9, Grade 1).
   - Attributed to treatment-related bone marrow suppression and chronic disease. Stable.
   - No transfusion indicated. Iron studies to be rechecked if further decline.

5. Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 61, AST 55).
   - Attributed to hepatic metastatic disease; trending appropriately with treatment response (trended down from baseline). No dose modification required.
   - Will recheck at next visit.

6. Diabetes mellitus type 2 (diet-controlled).
   - HbA1c not checked today; check at next scheduled visit. Dexamethasone premedications at each cycle may transiently impact glucose; patient educated on monitoring.

7. Advance care planning / goals of care.
   - Patient maintains goal of curative or maximal disease control. Well-informed regarding the complexity of achieving R0 resection in bilobar disease.
   - Discussed realistic probability of surgical candidacy at end of 12 cycles. Patient understands surgical window may close if further toxicity or progression occurs.
   - Palliative care team introduction offered; patient defers at this time but open to future referral.

Return visit: Cycle 9 Day 1 (in 2 weeks). Labs prior to each infusion. Formal end-of-induction imaging and tumor board review after Cycle 12.

Time spent: 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
Cosigned by [Fellow, Hematology/Oncology]
